title: ResizeObserver-based per-element width tracking that doesn't cause layout thrash
viewport_spectrum: Any element on any device — observed element widths vary independently of viewport
scenario_context: A chart component needs to know its container's exact pixel width to render an SVG with the right number of data points. Container width depends on flex/grid context, not viewport. ResizeObserver is the right tool, but naive implementations cause infinite re-render loops or layout thrash.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Setting state in ResizeObserver callback without check (loop)
Multiple observers per component (memory leak)
Reading getBoundingClientRect in observer callback (forced reflow)
Observer that updates parent layout based on its own size (infinite loop)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Container size != viewport size. Charts in sidebars, modals, accordions need their own size signal.\n2. ResizeObserver fires AFTER layout — safe to measure. But setting state triggers re-render which triggers another layout.\n3. Throttle via rAF + check if value actually changed before setState.

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: ResizeObserver with rAF throttle and value diffing
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default to 0 or estimated width server-side; client measures after mount
detection_strategy.code_pattern: const ro = new ResizeObserver((entries) => { rAF(() => setSize(entries[0].contentRect.width)); });

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Custom hook wrapping ResizeObserver with throttle
layout_strategy.content_priority: Chart adapts data point count to width
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Wider = more data points visible

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Chart adjusts to swiping container

performance_notes: rAF coalesces multiple resize events into a single state update. Value diffing prevents needless re-renders when size doesn't materially change. Single observer per component instance, properly cleaned up on unmount.
accessibility_across_viewports: SVG has role=img and aria-label describing point count. Each data circle could be focusable for detail (not shown). Chart renders only when width > 0 (post-measure), so no zero-sized first paint.
edge_cases_handled: Container in a flex layout that resizes — observer fires, chart re-renders with new point count
Component unmounts during rAF — cancelled cleanly, no setState on unmounted
Multiple instances on page — each has own observer, no contention
Component inside hidden tab — ResizeObserver pauses; resumes when visible
Container width briefly 0 during transition — guarded by width > 0 check
what_breaks_naive_approach: Setting state on every ResizeObserver fire without value diffing causes ReactDevTools to show 50+ renders/second during smooth resize. Without rAF throttle, observer callbacks fire multiple times per frame causing layout thrash. Observers that aren't cleaned up leak when component unmounts.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Element-level resize tracking with proper throttling — no layout thrash. 2) Value diffing prevents state churn. 3) Chart adapts data density to container, not viewport.

title: Card component that flips between vertical and horizontal layout based on container aspect ratio
viewport_spectrum: Card in tall narrow slot (220x400), short wide slot (400x150), square slot (300x300), large landscape (800x300)
scenario_context: A media tile shows poster image + title + metadata. In tall slots it should be vertical (image on top). In wide slots it should be horizontal (image on left). Aspect ratio of the slot determines orientation, not viewport size.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Width-only breakpoints (doesn't handle 800x300 differently from 800x800)
Forking component into <VerticalCard /> and <HorizontalCard />
Using JS to measure aspect ratio (use container queries)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Container aspect-ratio decides orientation. CSS container queries support aspect-ratio queries natively.\n2. Single component, two layouts via container aspect ratio.\n3. Image and text rebalance based on available space.

detection_strategy.code_pattern: container-type: inline-size doesn't suffice for aspect — use container-type: size for both dims

layout_strategy.primary_technique: container-type: size + @container aspect-ratio queries
layout_strategy.content_priority: Image and metadata always visible; orientation flips
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Horizontal layout shows more metadata inline; vertical stacks them

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap card to open detail
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same plus hover state on trackpad

performance_notes: Pure CSS. container-type: size queries both width and height. Single layout pass when container resizes.
accessibility_across_viewports: article landmark. Image with role=img + aria-label. Truncated text uses title attribute for full content on hover (not shown).
edge_cases_handled: Slot starts square then becomes wider — layout flips at 1:1 threshold
Container resize via parent grid — container query re-evaluates
Browser without container-type: size — falls back to default vertical layout
Image takes time to load — bg-cover prevents stretching
what_breaks_naive_approach: Width-only breakpoints can't distinguish 'wide and short' from 'wide and tall'. Two separate components fork maintenance and lose code reuse.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Aspect ratio drives layout, not just width. 2) Single component, two orientations. 3) container-type: size enables both width and height queries.

title: Display heading that uses font-size-adjust to maintain x-height consistency across web font swaps
viewport_spectrum: All viewports; concern is FOIT/FOUT during font load
scenario_context: A hero heading uses a custom font. While loading, the fallback (system sans-serif) renders at the same font-size but different x-height — visible jump when web font swaps in. font-size-adjust preserves x-height ratio so the swap is invisible.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: font-display: block (FOIT — invisible text)
No fallback metrics (jarring CLS on font swap)
Loading wrong weight via @font-face
Using web fonts for body without size-adjust descriptor
responsive_reasoning: 1. Font loading is a responsive-to-time concern. Fallback and loaded font must match VISUALLY.\n2. size-adjust descriptor in @font-face matches metrics between fallback and web font.\n3. font-size-adjust property on body provides additional safety net.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adjusted local font fallback with matching metrics
layout_strategy.content_priority: Text always visible (font-display: swap)
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: None

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: N/A

performance_notes: font-display: swap prevents FOIT — text always visible. Size-adjusted fallback prevents CLS on swap. Variable font (one file) covers all weights. Preloading critical font with <link rel='preload' as='font'> further reduces FOUT.
accessibility_across_viewports: Text always visible — no FOIT means screen readers and visual users get text immediately. Metrics match means no visual jump distracting users.
edge_cases_handled: Slow network — fallback renders identically-shaped text, swap is invisible
Network failure — fallback persists permanently, layout intact
User has disabled web fonts — fallback used, no broken state
User on Save-Data — preload skipped, fallback used
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default fallback (e.g., Times) has different x-height — when web font swaps, paragraphs reflow visibly (CLS hit), users lose reading position. size-adjust descriptor solves this fundamentally.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Layout-stable font loading via size-adjust. 2) Variable font reduces requests. 3) Progressive enhancement via fonts-loaded class for OpenType features.

title: Section padding that uses different fluid curves for vertical vs horizontal axes
viewport_spectrum: Mobile portrait (cramped vertical), mobile landscape (cramped horizontal), tablet (balanced), desktop (generous both)
scenario_context: Sections need different vertical and horizontal padding behavior. Vertical: should grow slower (we don't want sections too tall on mobile). Horizontal: should grow faster (mobile needs minimal side gutters). Single padding token doesn't capture this nuance.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Same padding token horizontal and vertical
Hard breakpoints with different values per side
Forgetting orientation (landscape phone has different needs)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Horizontal space and vertical space behave differently per viewport. Square padding ignores this.\n2. Two-axis fluid tokens — px-fluid and py-fluid with different clamp curves.\n3. Container shape (aspect ratio) further informs distribution.

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: clamp() with vw and vh respectively

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Separate horizontal/vertical fluid tokens
layout_strategy.content_priority: Vertical density preserved on mobile; horizontal gutters minimal
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: More content above fold on mobile; more breathing room on desktop

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Compact vertical means more content visible

performance_notes: Pure CSS, no JS. Single computed value per viewport.
accessibility_across_viewports: Spacing supports comfortable reading at any size. Doesn't break at zoom 200%.
edge_cases_handled: Landscape phone — vertical padding reduced, doesn't waste precious vertical space
Foldable in tablet mode — proportional to actual width
User zooms 200% — rem-based clamps scale up
Very tall ultrawide monitor — vertical caps prevent absurd top/bottom space
what_breaks_naive_approach: Square 4rem padding on landscape phone (375px wide × 200px tall portion of viewport with keyboard) wastes 8rem of precious vertical space. Different axes need different curves.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Two-axis fluid spacing — horizontal and vertical scale differently. 2) Orientation-aware overrides for landscape phone. 3) Aligned with content density needs per axis.

title: Tooltip that becomes a popover on touch and a description on screen readers
viewport_spectrum: Any device — issue is interaction model not size
scenario_context: Help tooltips on form labels work via hover on desktop. On touch, hover doesn't exist — users can't see help text. Long-press is unintuitive. Solution: tooltip on hover (desktop), tap-to-toggle popover (touch), always-available aria-describedby for screen readers.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: title attribute (no touch support, browser-controlled UI)
Hover-only tooltip (inaccessible on touch)
Long-press as the only mobile alternative (no discoverability)
Hiding tooltip text from screen readers
responsive_reasoning: 1. Three audiences: hover users (mouse), tap users (touch), screen reader users. Each needs the description accessible.\n2. aria-describedby connects label to description for all assistive tech.\n3. Visual presentation differs: hover-driven tooltip vs tap-driven popover.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: SSR-safe: description always in DOM, hidden visually

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Description always in DOM (sr-only by default), revealed via hover or click
layout_strategy.content_priority: Description always accessible to ATs; visible on demand
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: None

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap (i) icon → popover appears, tap outside or on icon to close
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Same as mobile or hover if trackpad
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Hover icon → tooltip appears, mouse away → fades
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Focus icon (via Tab) → tooltip appears, Escape dismisses, focus-visible parity

performance_notes: Description always rendered (sr-only when closed) — no async appearance. Single popover/tooltip strategy. Click outside to close (not shown) prevents stuck open state.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-describedby links description to field — screen readers announce 'Tax ID, more information available' and read description on focus. role=tooltip semantically correct. aria-expanded reflects state. Focus-visible parity ensures keyboard users see same visual reveal as mouse hover.
edge_cases_handled: Touch user taps icon while another tooltip open — only one open at a time via state
Keyboard user Tab to icon → tooltip appears via focus, persists until Tab away or Escape
Screen reader navigation — description read as part of field's accessible name/description, no extra interaction needed
Tooltip near viewport edge — could use floating-ui for collision avoidance (production improvement)
what_breaks_naive_approach: title attribute doesn't appear on touch — info is invisible to mobile users. Hover-only tooltip locks out keyboard users (focus parity missing). Tooltip text hidden from screen readers (display:none on tooltip) means SR users have NO way to access it.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Three input modalities, one component, one source of truth. 2) Screen reader has access regardless of visual state. 3) Focus-visible parity ensures keyboard users have same experience as mouse hover.

title: Scroll-into-view on focused input that survives address bar collapse and keyboard appearance
viewport_spectrum: iOS Safari (address bar collapses on scroll), Android Chrome (keyboard pushes content), iPad with floating keyboard
scenario_context: When user focuses a form field in a long form, that field should stay visible — not hidden behind keyboard or browser chrome. Naive scrollIntoView({block: 'center'}) doesn't account for keyboard. Need to compute the ACTUAL visible region and scroll to keep input within it.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: scrollIntoView without delay (fires before keyboard animates)
scrollIntoView with block: 'start' (input pinned to top — keyboard hides it)
Using window.scrollTo with hardcoded offsets
Ignoring visualViewport for visible-region calculation
responsive_reasoning: 1. 'Visible region' is not the viewport — it's viewport minus keyboard minus address bar.\n2. visualViewport.height gives the answer post-keyboard.\n3. Delay scrollIntoView until after keyboard animation completes (~300ms).

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: visualViewport.height after focus event + small delay

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Focus event + delayed scroll + visualViewport-aware center
layout_strategy.content_priority: Focused input always visible in keyboard-aware visible region
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: None

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap input → keyboard appears → field scrolls into view above keyboard
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Mouse focus on input — no keyboard, scrollIntoView positions normally
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab to input — scroll keeps it visible

performance_notes: Single event listener via event delegation on form. 300ms delay matches typical keyboard animation. scrollBy is GPU-accelerated.
accessibility_across_viewports: Doesn't break keyboard-only navigation (Tab through form scrolls naturally). Screen reader focus events trigger same scroll (often desired).
edge_cases_handled: User Tab through fields rapidly — last focus wins (setTimeout serializes)
Field already fully visible — no scroll triggered
Field above current view — scrolls up to center
iPad with floating keyboard — visualViewport reports correct visible region
External Bluetooth keyboard — desktop path used (no visualViewport keyboard inset)
what_breaks_naive_approach: scrollIntoView({block: 'center'}) fires BEFORE iOS keyboard animation completes — calculates center based on full window height, ends up positioning field behind keyboard. The 300ms delay is critical.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) visualViewport-aware visible region calculation. 2) Delayed scroll allows keyboard animation to complete. 3) Different code paths for touch keyboard vs physical keyboard (avoids unnecessary scroll on desktop).

title: Floating action button positioned above home indicator without overlapping content
viewport_spectrum: iPhone with home indicator, iPad with home indicator, Android with gesture pill, iPhone in landscape
scenario_context: An FAB (floating action button) at bottom-right of a list view. On iPhones with home indicator, naive bottom: 16px puts the FAB partially under the indicator. Need bottom positioning that respects safe areas dynamically.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: bottom: 16px hardcoded
Forgetting safe-area-inset-right in landscape
FAB that obscures critical content (need scroll padding to compensate)
responsive_reasoning: 1. FAB respects safe area on bottom AND side (landscape).\n2. Scrollable content needs padding-bottom equal to FAB height + safe area, so FAB doesn't permanently obscure last item.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Fixed FAB with safe-area offsets, content scroll padding compensates
layout_strategy.content_priority: FAB always reachable, no content permanently hidden
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Last items have extra scroll room

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap FAB (56pt) to trigger primary action
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: FAB pattern uncommon on desktop but supported
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: FAB is keyboard accessible via Tab; appears in last position

performance_notes: Pure CSS positioning. No JS. transform on active for tap feedback (GPU).
accessibility_across_viewports: 56pt FAB exceeds 44pt minimum. aria-label provides accessible name. Focus-visible ring not shown but recommended. List padding prevents last item from being hidden behind FAB.
edge_cases_handled: Landscape iPhone — FAB shifts left to avoid home indicator area on right
Tablet without home indicator — env values 0, max() ensures 16px floor
Long list — last item reachable via scroll because padding compensates
FAB on a page with no safe areas (desktop) — falls back to 16px
what_breaks_naive_approach: Fixed bottom: 16px on iPhone puts FAB half-overlapping the home indicator — tapping it might trigger system gesture instead. Without scroll padding, the last item in a list is permanently obscured by the FAB.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Safe-area-aware positioning. 2) Content padding compensates so FAB doesn't hide content. 3) Works in any orientation.

title: Reading mode that switches column count based on viewport aspect ratio and font size
viewport_spectrum: Tall portrait (1 column), wide landscape (2 columns), ultra-wide (3 columns), user with 200% zoom (1 column even on wide)
scenario_context: An e-reader app. On portrait phone, single column. On landscape tablet, two columns like a book. On ultrawide, three columns. But if user has large font (200% zoom), force single column regardless — columns become unreadably narrow.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Width-only breakpoints (ignores zoom)
Hard-coded column counts (doesn't adapt to font size)
CSS columns property without column-rule (no visual separation)
Columns that break across words
responsive_reasoning: 1. Column-count should respect minimum readable column width (ch units), not just viewport width.\n2. Use column-width instead of column-count — browser computes count based on width and min column-width.\n3. Respect prefers-reduced-data and user font size implicitly via ch-based math.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Available width relative to min column width

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS multi-column with column-width (not column-count)
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Continuous — column count emerges from width
layout_strategy.content_priority: Readable column width takes precedence over column count
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: More columns when space allows, never at expense of readability

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Swipe horizontally to next 'page' of columns
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Three columns, click anywhere to navigate

performance_notes: Pure CSS multi-column — browser handles flow. break-inside on paragraphs prevents awkward splits. No JS.
accessibility_across_viewports: Reading order preserved (top to bottom, then column to column — semantic). Text size respects user preference via ch units. Screen readers read content in DOM order regardless of visual columns.
edge_cases_handled: User zoom 200% — ch is relative to current font size, columns get wider effectively, fewer columns fit
Very wide screen — many columns automatically
Image inside text — break-inside-avoid keeps image with caption
Headings — break-after-avoid keeps heading with following paragraph
what_breaks_naive_approach: column-count: 3 forces 3 columns even on 320px phone — each column is ~100px, unreadable. column-width: 32ch lets browser compute — phone gets 1 column, tablet 2, desktop 3, automatic.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) column-width respects readability (ch-based) — column count emerges automatically. 2) Works at any zoom level because ch scales with font size. 3) Break controls prevent awkward splits.

title: Tablet keyboard shortcut hints that appear when keyboard attached, disappear when detached
viewport_spectrum: iPad with Magic Keyboard, iPad without keyboard, Surface Pro with/without keyboard, hybrid laptops in tablet mode
scenario_context: A productivity app shows keyboard shortcuts on hover (desktop) and in command palette. On iPad with Magic Keyboard, shortcuts ARE useful — user has physical keyboard. Without keyboard, shortcuts are noise. Detect keyboard attachment dynamically.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Device type sniffing (iPad has many input states)
Showing 'Ctrl+S' on touch-only devices (confusing)
Hiding shortcuts entirely on tablet (misses iPad+keyboard users)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Keyboard hint relevance is INPUT-dependent, not device-dependent.\n2. (any-hover: hover) and (any-pointer: fine) suggests keyboard/precision pointer attached.\n3. (pointer: coarse) primary still means touch-first, but any-* queries detect ADDITIONAL inputs.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Whether any precision pointer is attached

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS visibility via any-pointer media query
layout_strategy.content_priority: Same actions; shortcut hints conditional
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Hints add density when relevant

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: No shortcut hints
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Hints emphasized; full shortcut help available

performance_notes: Pure CSS for visual hint toggle. JS hook only for cases requiring JS knowledge (e.g., binding global shortcuts). matchMedia events fire only on input attachment/detachment.
accessibility_across_viewports: Hints use semantic <kbd> element. Screen readers may announce 'Save, keyboard shortcut Control S' for users who want it. When no keyboard, hints aren't rendered (no noise for touch users).
edge_cases_handled: iPad user attaches Magic Keyboard — any-pointer changes, hints appear
User detaches keyboard — hints disappear, no broken shortcuts
Surface Pro flipped to tablet mode — keyboard physically disconnected, hints hide
User with Bluetooth keyboard on iPhone — any-pointer:fine becomes true, hints appear
Multi-input device (touchscreen laptop) — any-pointer:fine is true (because of trackpad), hints shown
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hiding shortcuts on all touch devices misses iPad+keyboard users who actively use shortcuts. Showing them on touch-only iPad confuses users (Ctrl key doesn't exist). any-pointer media query is the precise signal.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Input-attachment-aware, not device-class-aware. 2) Reactive to attachment changes mid-session. 3) Same component, presence-aware presentation.

title: Master-detail with persistent URL state that survives viewport changes (deep linking works)
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (master OR detail page-by-page), tablet (master+detail side by side), desktop (same)
scenario_context: An admin panel has user list (master) and user detail. Deep link to /users/abc-123 should: on mobile, show only detail with back button. On tablet/desktop, show list + detail with abc-123 highlighted. URL is the source of truth.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: State in component (lost on refresh)
Different routes per viewport (/users vs /users/abc — confusing)
Same URL gives different UX based on user device without indication
responsive_reasoning: 1. URL is viewport-independent state. UI presentation is viewport-dependent.\n2. /users → list (mobile shows just list, desktop empty detail pane).\n3. /users/:id → detail (mobile shows just detail, desktop shows list highlighted + detail).

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: URL is SSR-friendly; layout decided by useBreakpoint client-side
detection_strategy.code_pattern: if (mobile && id) return <Detail />; if (mobile) return <List />; return <ListAndDetail />;

layout_strategy.primary_technique: URL-driven content, viewport-driven layout
layout_strategy.content_priority: URL determines what's shown; viewport determines how
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Tablet+ shows both panes; mobile shows one at a time

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap list item → navigate to /users/abc → detail page, back button → /users
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Tap list item → URL updates, detail pane updates, list stays visible
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Same as tablet plus hover preview
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Arrow keys navigate list, URL updates on each

performance_notes: Server components render detail content; client component handles layout decisions. URL is the source of truth — refresh works, back button works, shareable links work. No state synchronization needed.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-current=page on active list item. Mobile back button has aria-label. Real Link elements (not buttons) — keyboard nav works natively. Heading hierarchy preserved across viewports.
edge_cases_handled: User shares link /users/abc-123 — opens to detail on mobile, master-detail on desktop
User on tablet rotates to portrait phone — layout swaps but URL stays, detail still shown
User clicks list item, navigates to detail, hits back — URL changes to /users, list shown
Deep refresh on detail page — SSR works, no flash
what_breaks_naive_approach: State-driven (not URL-driven) navigation means refresh breaks, shareable links don't exist, back button confuses. Different routes per viewport (e.g., /m/users/abc vs /users/abc) creates code duplication.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) URL is viewport-independent — deep links work everywhere. 2) Single route, two viewport presentations. 3) Back button matches user mental model on mobile, list stays visible on desktop.

title: Sortable, filterable table whose controls collapse into a single trigger on mobile
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (filter button → sheet), tablet (inline filter chips), desktop (full toolbar)
scenario_context: A table with 8 sortable columns, 3 filterable fields, and a search input. On desktop the toolbar shows everything. On tablet, search inline + 'More filters' button. On mobile, single 'Filter' button opens bottom sheet with all controls.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hide-and-show buttons (loses functionality on mobile)
Different filter components per viewport (state drift)
Filter sheet without restore-on-open
responsive_reasoning: 1. Controls have same data semantics; presentation depends on viewport.\n2. Filter state lives in URL or context — viewport change preserves filters.\n3. Mobile sheet uses full screen real estate for filter UI; desktop uses inline controls.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Server renders filters from URL params

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adaptive control component, shared filter state
layout_strategy.content_priority: Search > filters > sort > columns visibility
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Desktop shows all; mobile hides until requested

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap 'Filter' button → sheet → adjust → apply
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab through controls; Cmd+F focuses search

performance_notes: Filter state lives in parent — single source. Sheet only mounts when open. Search input is debounced in production for filter performance.
accessibility_across_viewports: Sheet has role=dialog aria-modal. Filter button shows count via badge (visually) AND in aria-label (announcement). Search has aria-label and type=search (clear button native).
edge_cases_handled: User applies filters on desktop, resizes to mobile — filter state preserved in URL/context
User taps Filter button while another sheet open — close existing first (state handles)
Filters with many options — sheet scrolls internally
Apply button always accessible (sticky bottom)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hiding controls behind 'Show filters' link on mobile without sheet creates a long inline form pushing the table off screen. Showing all controls in toolbar at 375px wraps to 4 rows of buttons.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same filter state, two presentations (toolbar/sheet). 2) Mobile sheet uses full screen for clarity. 3) Filter count badge gives mobile users awareness without revealing detail.

title: Address form with auto-complete and country-aware field layout
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (single column, native pickers), desktop (logical grid layout, country-specific fields)
scenario_context: Address forms vary by country: US has state dropdown + ZIP, UK has postcode (free text), Germany has separate house number. Layout should adapt to country selection AND viewport. Single column mobile; for desktop, grid that matches the country's address format.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Same fields for all countries (loses local correctness)
Desktop layout that ignores country (US-centric default)
Forgetting autoComplete (Apple Pay can't fill, password managers can't help)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Address fields are LOCALLY correct, not universally. Country drives field set.\n2. Layout grid varies — US is City+State+ZIP row, UK is City row + Postcode row.\n3. autoComplete tokens (address-line1, postal-code) enable browser autofill regardless of layout.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default country from i18n; user selects country first

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Per-country field configuration + per-viewport grid template
layout_strategy.content_priority: Country first; then country-specific fields
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Desktop shows fields in logical groupings; mobile stacks

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Country dropdown native, ZIP/postcode triggers numeric inputMode

performance_notes: Single form re-render on country change. autoComplete tokens enable browser/password-manager autofill. inputMode='numeric' triggers numeric keyboard on iOS for US ZIP.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real labels associated with inputs. autoComplete tokens improve screen reader context. Country select is native. Logical Tab order respects country layout.
edge_cases_handled: User changes country mid-fill — fields re-arrange but values preserved per field name
Apple Pay autofill — autoComplete tokens guide which fields to fill
US ZIP with letters (rare) — type='text' allows it; inputMode hints numeric for keyboard
Mobile rotation — single column persists, no layout shift
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded US layout for all countries breaks German addresses (no separate house number) and shows 'State' for UK users (no such concept). autoComplete='off' breaks password managers and Apple Pay autofill — massive UX regression.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Country-aware field set AND layout. 2) Viewport-aware grid topology — German house number inline on desktop, stacked on mobile. 3) Proper autoComplete tokens enable native autofill across all viewports.

title: Lazy-loaded image gallery with native loading + IntersectionObserver fallback and aspect-ratio reservation
viewport_spectrum: Long pages on any viewport; especially low-bandwidth mobile where data savings matter most
scenario_context: A long image-heavy page. Modern browsers support loading='lazy' natively. Older browsers don't. Need a pattern that uses native where possible, IntersectionObserver fallback, and never has CLS from images loading.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Eager loading all images (massive initial payload)
loading='lazy' without explicit width/height (layout shift)
IntersectionObserver per image (too many observers)
Lazy load with no placeholder (blank gaps during scroll)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Native lazy loading is opt-in (loading attribute) — modern browsers respect, older ignore.\n2. aspect-ratio CSS prevents CLS regardless of load timing.\n3. Shared IntersectionObserver pattern for fallback (single observer, many targets).

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: loading='lazy' attribute + shared IntersectionObserver for older browsers
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Pure HTML for SSR images; JS enhancement for fallback

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Native loading=lazy + aspect-ratio + LQIP placeholder
layout_strategy.content_priority: Above-fold eager, below-fold lazy
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: None

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Scroll triggers lazy load

performance_notes: Single IntersectionObserver across all images (vs one per image — saves memory). rootMargin: '200px' starts loading before image enters viewport — feels eager visually. LQIP (low-quality image placeholder) provides instant visual feedback. aspect-ratio prevents CLS regardless of loading state.
accessibility_across_viewports: alt text always required. Decorative placeholder is aria-hidden. Loading state is visual only — assistive tech sees the final alt text.
edge_cases_handled: Browser without loading=lazy support — IntersectionObserver fallback kicks in
User has slow connection — placeholder shown until image loads
User scrolls quickly past — image still loads (rootMargin prefetches)
Image fails to load — alt text shown, layout preserved by aspect-ratio
Print stylesheet — could eager-load all images for print
what_breaks_naive_approach: loading='lazy' without width/height causes images to load with 0x0 reserved space, then push content down on load — terrible CLS. Eager-loading 50 images at page load costs ~10MB on a long page. Per-image observers leak memory on long pages.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Native loading=lazy primary, IntersectionObserver fallback. 2) aspect-ratio reservation = zero CLS regardless of load state. 3) Single shared observer for memory efficiency.

title: Breadcrumbs that truncate intelligently with overflow menu on small viewports
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (Home > ... > Current), tablet (Home > Parent > Current), desktop (full path)
scenario_context: A deeply-nested admin app shows breadcrumbs: Home > Settings > Billing > Subscriptions > Plan Details. On mobile this wraps to 3 lines and looks awful. Need truncation pattern: show first + last on mobile, with '...' that opens dropdown showing hidden items.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hide breadcrumbs on mobile entirely (lose navigation context)
Truncate text within each crumb (loses meaning)
Show all crumbs wrapping (ugly, takes space from content)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Breadcrumbs serve same purpose at every size — show ancestry. Visual density adapts.\n2. First and last are most important (home + current). Middle is collapsible.\n3. Overflow menu (...) makes hidden ancestors accessible.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Server renders all crumbs; CSS hides via container queries

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Render all crumbs server-side; CSS hides middle ones via container query; JS handles overflow menu
layout_strategy.fluid_vs_breakpoint: Categorical for show/hide; fluid for spacing
layout_strategy.content_priority: First (home) + last (current) always visible; middle in overflow
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile collapses middle into one '...' menu

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap '...' button → dropdown with hidden crumbs
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Some middle crumbs visible, '...' for the rest if needed

performance_notes: Server-rendered; client-only JS for overflow menu toggle. Container queries handle visibility — no JS measurement.
accessibility_across_viewports: nav with aria-label='Breadcrumb'. aria-current='page' on current item. Overflow button has aria-label and aria-expanded. Overflow menu uses role=menu/menuitem. Hidden crumbs are display:none via container query — screen readers still see them in DOM order if read at element level, but the menu surface is the discoverable path.
edge_cases_handled: Very long crumb labels — truncate with title attribute (production)
Single-level page (just current) — no chevron, no overflow
User changes viewport — same DOM, CSS toggles visibility
Keyboard user — can Tab into overflow menu when at small size
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hiding breadcrumbs entirely on mobile loses navigation context. Wrapping all crumbs creates 3-line nav stealing content space. Truncating each crumb's text makes labels meaningless ('Subs...' < 'Subscriptions').
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) First + last always visible (most important crumbs). 2) Middle accessible via overflow menu on mobile. 3) Single component, container-query driven.

title: Dropdown menu that becomes action sheet on mobile, with same trigger and same items
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (action sheet from bottom), tablet/desktop (anchored dropdown)
scenario_context: A 'More actions' (three-dot) menu on each row of a list. On desktop it's a small anchored dropdown. On mobile it's a full-width action sheet from bottom for thumb-reach. Same trigger, same items, different presentation.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Tiny dropdown on mobile (hard to tap)
Different components for the two presentations (state drift)
Action sheet without backdrop
Anchored dropdown that goes off-screen on tablet
responsive_reasoning: 1. Mobile users have thumbs — small anchored dropdowns are unreachable. Action sheet from bottom is thumb-friendly.\n2. Same items, same handlers; container differs.\n3. Anchor-aware dropdown for desktop respects available space.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adaptive container component with shared items
layout_strategy.content_priority: Same items across; presentation maximizes accessibility per viewport
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Sheet items larger (touch); dropdown items compact (mouse)

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap trigger → sheet rises from bottom, tap item, sheet dismisses
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab to trigger, Enter opens, arrow keys navigate items, Enter selects, Escape closes

performance_notes: Portal rendering avoids z-index issues. Position calculated once on open, not during scroll (good for performance, fine for one-time open). Use floating-ui for production for collision detection.
accessibility_across_viewports: Trigger has aria-haspopup=menu, aria-expanded. Container has role=menu, items role=menuitem. Escape closes. Focus management not shown (focus first item on open is recommended). Cancel button on mobile sheet matches iOS convention.
edge_cases_handled: Dropdown near right edge — left calc keeps it on-screen
Mobile sheet items > screen height — would need internal scroll (production)
User triggers menu on row while another menu open — state-based, only one at a time per parent
Keyboard user — Tab to trigger, Enter opens (focus to menu — to be added)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Small anchored dropdown on mobile means user has to tap a precise 80px wide area with a thumb — high error rate. Different components per viewport means menu items could drift between presentations.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same items, two presentation modes — sheet for thumbs, dropdown for cursors. 2) Sheet items are 56pt tall (touch-friendly). 3) Anchor-aware dropdown stays on-screen.

title: Old phones still in use: Galaxy S8 (360px), iPhone 8 (375px), low-power devices with limited RAM
viewport_spectrum: Galaxy S8 360x640, iPhone 8 375x667, low-end Android with 2GB RAM, slow GPUs (Mali-T880)
scenario_context: 10% of users still use phones from 2017-2019 era. These devices have small viewports, slower CPUs, and limited RAM. Modern designs that work on iPhone 15 may break on these. Need to design for the long tail.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Assuming min mobile width 375px (S8 is 360px)
Heavy animations on low-end GPUs (jank)
Loading all images at full resolution
Bundling 500KB JS for a simple page
responsive_reasoning: 1. Lower viewport bound is 320px (iPhone SE 1st gen) or 360px (Galaxy S8) — not 375.\n2. Low-end devices benefit from prefers-reduced-motion respect and lazy loading.\n3. JS bundle size matters more here — parse/compile is slow.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: SSR rendered HTML works offline of JS
detection_strategy.code_pattern: px-3 sm:px-4 md:px-6 — start with minimal mobile padding

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Mobile-first with 320px lower bound
layout_strategy.content_priority: Essential content readable at 320px
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Lowest end shows minimum viable density

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: 44pt minimum even on 360px screen

performance_notes: Critical bundle <50KB for these devices. Defer analytics, ads, social widgets. Use loading='lazy' aggressively. Avoid backdrop-filter (slow on Mali GPUs).
accessibility_across_viewports: 44pt touch targets even at 360px width. Font sizes legible at 14px minimum. Color contrast 4.5:1 minimum.
edge_cases_handled: Galaxy S8 at 360px — content fits without horizontal scroll
User on slow CPU — minimal JS to parse on first load
User with 2GB RAM — small bundle, no memory pressure
Older browser (Chrome 90 era) — no use of cutting-edge CSS features without fallback
what_breaks_naive_approach: Designing for iPhone 15 (393px) breaks Galaxy S8 (360px) — 33px difference is one tap target. Heavy animations on Mali-T880 GPU cause 10fps scrolling.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) 320px lower bound, not 375. 2) Performance-conscious for low-end CPUs. 3) Lazy-loaded everything below the fold.

title: DPR-aware canvas rendering for retina-quality charts without performance penalty on standard displays
viewport_spectrum: 1x DPR (most desktops), 2x DPR (Retina Macs), 3x DPR (iPhones), variable DPR (when user zooms or moves windows between monitors)
scenario_context: An analytics chart drawn to <canvas>. On 1x display, render at canvas size. On 2x/3x Retina, render at backing store resolution to avoid blur. Must handle DPR changes when user drags window from Retina to non-Retina display.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Ignoring DPR (blurry on Retina)
Setting canvas backing store too large always (wastes GPU memory on 1x)
Hard-coding DPR (changes when user moves monitor)
Forgetting CSS-size vs backing-store-size distinction
responsive_reasoning: 1. DPR is invisible viewport context — affects rendering quality.\n2. CSS size = display size (in CSS pixels). Backing store = pixel buffer (device pixels).\n3. Watch matchMedia('(resolution: ...)') for DPR changes.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default DPR 1 on server; client adjusts on mount
detection_strategy.code_pattern: canvas.width = cssWidth * dpr; canvas.height = cssHeight * dpr; ctx.scale(dpr, dpr);

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Canvas backing-store size = CSS size × DPR
layout_strategy.content_priority: Sharp rendering at any DPR
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Same content; pixel density adapts

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Sharp on retina iPhones
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Adapts when window moves between monitors

performance_notes: Backing store scales with DPR — 3x retina gets 9x pixels. Pre-computed once per resize/DPR change, not per frame. ctx.scale once applies to all subsequent draws.
accessibility_across_viewports: Canvas has role=img + aria-label. For data tables alternative, provide a hidden <table> for screen readers.
edge_cases_handled: User drags window from 2x Retina to 1x external monitor — DPR changes, hook fires, canvas re-renders sharp
User zooms browser — devicePixelRatio updates, redraws sharp
Canvas size 0 (during initial mount) — guarded by width === 0 check
Very large DPR (5x for some Android phones) — works, just consumes more memory
what_breaks_naive_approach: canvas.width = 800 on 2x display draws at 800px backing store but CSS displays at 800px logical = blurry. Setting backing store too large always wastes GPU memory on 1x displays. Not watching matchMedia means moving between monitors leaves blurry rendering.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) DPR-aware canvas backing store — sharp on any display. 2) Reactive to DPR changes (multi-monitor setups). 3) Same drawing code works regardless of DPR.

title: Form layout that switches between stacked and inline based on container width, not viewport
viewport_spectrum: Form rendered in modals (400px), sidebars (320px), main content (800px), full-page (1200px) — at ANY viewport
scenario_context: A login form. When rendered in a narrow modal it should stack (email above, password above, button). When rendered in a wide page section it could be inline (email | password | button). The viewport doesn't matter — the slot does.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Viewport breakpoints (irrelevant — modal at 400px on 4K should still stack)
Forking form per context
Manual width prop
responsive_reasoning: 1. Form layout depends on its container's space, not viewport.\n2. Single form, container-query driven.\n3. Same form fields and submit button.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Container query switching grid template
layout_strategy.content_priority: Same fields; layout shifts
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Wide containers show fields side by side

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: N/A — container-driven
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Tab order respects DOM order regardless of visual layout

performance_notes: Pure CSS. Single layout pass on container resize.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real form, real labels, autoComplete tokens. Tab order is email → password → submit (matches DOM). Submit button is real button.
edge_cases_handled: Form in 320px sidebar — stacks, button full-width
Form in 1200px section — inline, button hugs content
Modal that resizes — form adapts in real-time
Same form in two contexts on the same page — each adapts independently
what_breaks_naive_approach: Viewport breakpoints can't distinguish 'form in narrow modal on desktop' from 'form full-width on mobile' — both might be 400px wide but viewport differs. Container queries are the correct tool.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Form adapts to its slot, not viewport. 2) Same component works in modals, sidebars, full pages. 3) Pure CSS.

title: Heading sizes that respect their HEADING LEVEL semantically, not just visual hierarchy
viewport_spectrum: Any viewport
scenario_context: An article has h1 (page title), h2 (sections), h3 (subsections). Each renders at a fluid size. But a 'h3 styled to look like h2' is bad practice — visual hierarchy should match semantic hierarchy. Type scale should map h1>h2>h3 strictly, with no overrides.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Custom classes like 'text-h2' applied to h4 (semantic-visual mismatch)
Inverting hierarchy (h3 larger than h2)
Same size for h2 and h3 (no hierarchy)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Heading semantics indicate document structure for screen readers AND SEO.\n2. Visual hierarchy MUST match semantic hierarchy.\n3. CSS resets headings to consistent fluid scale; never override per-component.

detection_strategy.how_to_detect: CSS custom properties + heading element selectors
detection_strategy.code_pattern: h1 { font-size: var(--step-6); } h2 { font-size: var(--step-5); } /* etc */

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Heading element styles globally; no component-level override
layout_strategy.content_priority: h1 > h2 > h3 always
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Hierarchy maintained at all sizes

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: N/A
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Screen reader heading nav (h key) jumps through hierarchy correctly

performance_notes: Pure CSS. Cascading custom properties resolved once. No runtime cost.
accessibility_across_viewports: Screen readers (NVDA, VoiceOver, JAWS) use heading hierarchy for navigation. Skipping levels (h1 → h3) is a WCAG violation. This system enforces correct levels through naming.
edge_cases_handled: Designer wants visually smaller h2 — use class heading-3 on <h2> element. Semantics preserved, visual flex.
User Tab to heading via h-key — proper hierarchy enables fast scanning
Print stylesheet — headings size in pt for print
Heading at end of narrow column — line-height tight prevents overflow
what_breaks_naive_approach: Applying heading sizes via per-component class (e.g., 'text-2xl' on a random div) creates inconsistent typography. Using h3 because 'h2 looks too big' breaks semantic hierarchy.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Semantic-first typography — element drives size, not classes. 2) Fluid scale across viewports. 3) Optional class overrides preserve semantic level when designer needs visual flexibility.

title: Stack layout primitive with fluid gap that respects container queries for adaptive density
viewport_spectrum: Any container — stack works at any size
scenario_context: A Stack primitive (vertical or horizontal flex with consistent gap) used throughout an app. Gap should be fluid but also adaptive to container — a Stack in a tight sidebar uses smaller gaps than one in a hero section.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Fixed gaps (don't scale)
Per-component gap overrides (loss of rhythm)
Tailwind gap-4 hardcoded (no fluidity)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Stack is a layout primitive — consumed by hundreds of components. Gap must be sensible everywhere.\n2. Container queries let Stack adapt to its space.\n3. Token-driven scale supports all use cases.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Stack with container-query-driven gap
layout_strategy.content_priority: Spacing density adapts to container
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Tight containers → tighter spacing

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: N/A — layout primitive

performance_notes: Pure CSS via container queries. Tokens centralized. Single primitive replaces hundreds of ad-hoc layout decisions.
accessibility_across_viewports: No accessibility concerns — pure layout primitive. Tab order respects DOM. Focus rings unaffected.
edge_cases_handled: Stack in 320px sidebar — gap auto-reduces
Stack in hero section — full gap
Nested stacks — each adapts to its own container
Stack with wrap — gap applies to wrap rows too
what_breaks_naive_approach: Hardcoded gap-4 on every Stack creates inconsistent rhythm and doesn't adapt to constrained contexts. Per-component overrides multiply complexity.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Single primitive, hundreds of consumers. 2) Container-aware gap. 3) Token-driven scale supports the entire design system.

title: Pull-to-refresh that doesn't trigger on mouse-scroll-up at top of page
viewport_spectrum: Touch devices (mobile/tablet)
scenario_context: A feed app supports pull-to-refresh. Touch users pull down at the top to refresh. Mouse users scrolling up at the top should NOT trigger refresh. Need pointer-type discrimination.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: scroll event-based pull-to-refresh (fires on mouse wheel too)
Always-on refresh (annoying on desktop)
Native iOS overscroll-behavior off (loses momentum)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Pull-to-refresh is a touch gesture, not a scroll behavior.\n2. Detect via PointerEvent.pointerType === 'touch'.\n3. Use overscroll-behavior CSS to prevent native bounce from interfering.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Touch pointer at top of scroll, pulling down

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Pointer event tracking + transform on container
layout_strategy.content_priority: Refresh indicator appears as user pulls
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: None

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Pull down at top → indicator appears → release at threshold to refresh
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Mouse wheel up at top → nothing happens (correct)

performance_notes: Pointer events unified. Transform-based animation (GPU). passive listeners where possible. overscroll-behavior prevents iOS rubber-band from breaking the gesture.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-live announces refresh state. Manual refresh button should always exist as accessible alternative. Refresh button (not shown) at top of view for keyboard users.
edge_cases_handled: Mouse user scrolls up at top — pointerType !== 'touch', no pull initiated
User pulls past threshold then back — no refresh until threshold met on release
User pulls slightly then scrolls list — damping makes pull soft, scroll takes over after release
iOS overscroll bounce — overscroll-behavior contains it
what_breaks_naive_approach: Listening to scroll events for pull-to-refresh fires on mouse wheel — desktop users get unexpected refresh. Not damping pull makes it feel infinite or stuttery.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Touch-only gesture via pointerType discrimination. 2) Damping makes pull feel natural. 3) Desktop unaffected — keyboard shortcut or button for that audience.

title: Multi-step form keyboard navigation: Next button updates with keyboard's enter, swipe gestures preserved
viewport_spectrum: iOS keyboard with done/next, Android with action button, hardware keyboard with Tab/Enter
scenario_context: A multi-step form. Each field has 'enter key hint' set so iOS shows 'Next' on last input of step → Enter submits the step. Combined with visualViewport keyboard handling, this creates fluid step-through typing.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Default 'Go' or 'Return' on every field
Not setting enterkeyhint (loses iOS hint)
Submit on Enter for all fields (premature)
responsive_reasoning: 1. enterkeyhint attribute lets iOS/Android customize keyboard's action key.\n2. 'next' moves to next field; 'done' closes keyboard; 'send' submits.\n3. Step navigation can be triggered from keyboard 'done' for fast progression.

detection_strategy.what_to_detect: Current field is last in step
detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Server renders all attributes; no JS needed for hints

layout_strategy.primary_technique: enterkeyhint per field + onSubmit handling
layout_strategy.content_priority: Same fields; keyboard chrome adapts
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: None

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap field → keyboard appears with appropriate action key → Tab/Done moves to next or completes step
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Enter on last field submits the form

performance_notes: Native HTML form submission. Single state update per field change. Focus management uses rAF to ensure DOM is settled.
accessibility_across_viewports: Real form with submit. enterkeyhint provides visual cue on virtual keyboards. Tab order natural. Required fields enforced by browser.
edge_cases_handled: iOS user taps 'Next' on keyboard — submits form via native Enter behavior, step advances
Hardware keyboard user — Tab/Enter works identically
User submits partially-filled — HTML required attribute catches missing fields with native validation
Step transition — focus moves to first field of new step via rAF
what_breaks_naive_approach: Default 'Return' key on every field gives no hint to mobile users about step structure. Submit-on-Enter without step awareness submits the entire form prematurely.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) enterkeyhint customizes virtual keyboard's action key per field. 2) Native form submission keeps it simple. 3) Step transitions focus first field automatically.

title: Modal that respects safe areas WITHIN the modal (not just outside)
viewport_spectrum: iPhone landscape with notch (left/right safe areas), iPad with home indicator (bottom)
scenario_context: A fullscreen modal on iPhone in landscape. The modal background extends edge-to-edge, but the close button on the right is hidden by the notch. Need internal padding using env() values so content stays in safe area.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Padding the parent (background also gets pushed in)
Fixed padding regardless of device
Forgetting safe areas inside modals
responsive_reasoning: 1. Modals can be full-bleed (background) but content must respect safe areas.\n2. env() inside modal content works just like top-level.\n3. Close button in particular needs to be reachable, not hidden.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Full-bleed modal with internal safe-area-aware content padding
layout_strategy.content_priority: Close button always reachable; content within safe zone
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Same content; positioning adapts to device chrome

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Close button reachable in any orientation
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Bottom of modal not blocked by home indicator
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Safe areas usually 0; standard padding applies
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Escape closes; Tab through modal contents

performance_notes: Pure CSS env(). 100dvh handles dynamic viewport for iOS Safari address bar.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=dialog aria-modal aria-label. Close button has aria-label and is 44pt. Focus trap not shown but required in production.
edge_cases_handled: iPhone landscape with notch on left — left padding shifts content right
iPad portrait with home indicator — bottom padding keeps controls accessible
Browser without viewport-fit=cover — env values are 0, max() provides minimum
Modal content scrolls — internal scroll preserves header
what_breaks_naive_approach: Padding the parent pushes background away from edges — modal no longer full-bleed. No safe-area awareness on close button means landscape iPhone users can't tap it.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Background full-bleed, content safe. 2) All four safe areas respected within modal. 3) max() provides floor padding even on devices with no safe areas.

title: Map view that switches between portrait (map + bottom sheet) and landscape (map + side panel)
viewport_spectrum: Phone portrait, phone landscape, tablet portrait, tablet landscape
scenario_context: A maps app. In portrait, map is on top, location details slide up from bottom as a sheet. In landscape (especially phone landscape with little vertical), details should be in a left/right panel instead — keeping map height usable.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Bottom sheet in landscape phone (consumes precious vertical)
Width-only detection (doesn't capture landscape phone)
Same UX everywhere
responsive_reasoning: 1. Landscape phone has tiny vertical (~375px). Bottom sheet eats 50% = 188px of map. Side panel is better.\n2. Orientation drives layout — same data, different placement.\n3. Side panel slides from left or right depending on safe area (notch side).

detection_strategy.code_pattern: @media (orientation: landscape) and (max-height: 500px) { .details { position: fixed; left: 0; width: 320px; height: 100dvh; } }

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Orientation-aware panel placement
layout_strategy.content_priority: Map always primary; details adapt placement
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Same details; sheet vs side panel

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Portrait: tap pin → sheet up. Landscape: tap pin → side panel slides in.
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Both orientations show side panel (more room)

performance_notes: Pure CSS layout. Grid template swaps based on orientation media query. Map element stays mounted (preserves view state).
accessibility_across_viewports: aside has implicit complementary role. Map should have role=application or img with description. Details panel scrollable independently.
edge_cases_handled: Phone rotates landscape → details slide from bottom sheet to side panel without remount, content preserved
iPad portrait → landscape — side panel grows wider (more room)
Notch on left in landscape — side panel respects safe-area-inset-left
Very wide ultrawide — side panel caps at 400px
what_breaks_naive_approach: Bottom sheet on landscape phone eats half the vertical space — user can barely see map. Side panel is the right answer for short-tall viewports.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Orientation drives panel placement intentionally. 2) Safe area handled per orientation. 3) Same component handles four distinct presentations.

title: Drawing tool with palette that adapts: floating mini-palette on phone, side panel on tablet, full toolbar on desktop
viewport_spectrum: Phone (compact floating tools), tablet (left side panel), desktop (top toolbar)
scenario_context: A drawing app on phone has limited canvas space. The tool palette should be a minimal floating dock — maybe 4-5 essential tools. On tablet, more tools in a left rail. On desktop, full toolbar at the top with all tools and submenus.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Cramming desktop toolbar on phone (eats canvas)
Hiding tools on phone (loses functionality)
Different tool sets per viewport (confusing)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Same tools, different presentation density and accessibility.\n2. Phone: floating dock with overflow menu. Tablet: vertical rail. Desktop: horizontal toolbar.\n3. State (selected tool, color) shared across.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Three palette variants with shared tool state
layout_strategy.content_priority: Canvas takes max space; palette minimizes footprint per viewport
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Tools count varies (5 on phone dock + overflow, 12 on tablet rail, all on desktop)

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap dock tool to select; long-press for color picker (haptic)
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Click tool, hover shows tooltip with shortcut

performance_notes: Single palette state. Conditional render of three variants — only one mounts at a time.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-pressed on tool buttons. aria-label on each. Keyboard shortcuts (via document keydown handler, not shown). Desktop tooltips show shortcuts.
edge_cases_handled: Phone overflow menu — opens above dock, dismisses on selection
Tablet rail — full height, scrollable if needed
Desktop toolbar — wraps to next line if many tools
User resizes mid-draw — selected tool preserved across variant swap
what_breaks_naive_approach: Desktop top toolbar on phone eats 50px from a 600px-tall canvas — unacceptable. Hiding tools entirely loses functionality.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Same tools, three presentations matching screen real estate. 2) Overflow handling on phone preserves all functionality. 3) Tool state shared across variants.

title: Slack-style three-column with workspace switcher, channels, and conversation that collapses gracefully
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (one column with bottom nav), tablet (two of three columns), desktop (all three)
scenario_context: A chat app like Slack: workspace rail (left, 60px), channels list (220px), conversation (rest). On phone, single column with bottom nav for workspace switching. On tablet, hide workspace rail, show channels + conversation. On desktop, all three.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Drop workspace rail without alternative (lose workspace switching)
Hamburger on every panel (cluttered)
Different conversation component per layout
responsive_reasoning: 1. Three levels of navigation hierarchy collapse asymmetrically per viewport.\n2. Workspace switching becomes bottom nav on mobile (different UI for same function).\n3. Channels list becomes a slide-out drawer on tablet/mobile.

detection_strategy.ssr_considerations: Default to desktop layout SSR for SEO; client adapts
detection_strategy.code_pattern: Three-pane on desktop, two-pane on tablet (workspace as drawer), single-pane on mobile (bottom nav for workspace)

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Hierarchical pane collapse with shared state
layout_strategy.content_priority: Conversation always visible (primary)
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile: conversation only; tablet: channels+conversation; desktop: all

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap channel name in header → channels drawer. Tap workspace icon in bottom nav → workspace drawer.
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Tap workspace icon in channels header → workspace drawer. Channels always visible.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: All three columns visible. Click to navigate.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Cmd+K command palette for fast nav

performance_notes: Each viewport renders only the panes it shows. Drawers portal-rendered. State for drawer open/close is local; conversation content lives outside (preserves message scroll).
accessibility_across_viewports: Drawer role=dialog aria-modal. nav with aria-label for bottom workspace nav. Menu buttons have aria-label. Focus management not shown (focus first interactive in drawer on open).
edge_cases_handled: User on mobile opens channels drawer, taps channel — drawer dismisses, conversation updates
Tablet user opens workspace drawer — overlays main UI, conversation preserved
Resize from desktop to mobile mid-message-compose — conversation stays open, surrounding nav adapts
Keyboard user — Tab navigates within current visible pane
what_breaks_naive_approach: Cramming three panes in 375px gives unusable column widths. Dropping workspace switching entirely on mobile is a critical feature loss.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Three navigation levels collapse asymmetrically — workspace becomes bottom nav, channels become drawer. 2) Conversation always visible — primary content protected. 3) Drawer pattern for surfaced-when-needed nav.

title: Bulk-actions toolbar that floats above keyboard, stays on screen during selection
viewport_spectrum: Mobile (floating bottom bar), tablet (inline above table), desktop (above table)
scenario_context: When user selects multiple rows in a table, a bulk-actions bar (Delete, Move, Archive) appears. On mobile this floats at bottom — but must stay above the iOS keyboard if the user has a search field open.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Hide actions toolbar when keyboard appears (loses bulk ops mid-task)
Position fixed without visualViewport awareness
Bar that obscures last selected row
responsive_reasoning: 1. Selection state is global; toolbar visible whenever count > 0.\n2. Mobile bottom positioning needs keyboard inset compensation.\n3. List padding compensates so last row visible above toolbar.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Conditional toolbar with visualViewport-aware positioning
layout_strategy.content_priority: Bulk actions always reachable while selection active
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Selection count + 3-5 primary actions

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap action button on floating bar
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Same as tablet plus keyboard shortcuts
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Cmd+A select all, Delete key for delete action

performance_notes: Renders only when selection active. visualViewport handler shared with form pattern. Single state update for selection count.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=region aria-label announces selection count. Each button has aria-label. Touch targets 44pt. Keyboard shortcuts apply (Delete key triggers delete on desktop).
edge_cases_handled: User mid-bulk action opens search input — keyboard appears, toolbar slides up to stay visible
User dismisses keyboard — toolbar returns to bottom
Selection cleared — toolbar slides out
Many actions — overflow into 'More' on very narrow viewports (not shown)
what_breaks_naive_approach: Toolbar with position: fixed bottom: 0 sits under keyboard when search opens — actions unreachable mid-task. Hiding toolbar when keyboard appears loses the user's selection context.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) visualViewport-aware positioning keeps toolbar above keyboard. 2) Different layouts per viewport (icons-only mobile, full text desktop). 3) Selection state persists across viewport changes.

title: Password input with strength meter and visibility toggle that maintains layout at all sizes
viewport_spectrum: 320px to 1920px
scenario_context: Password field needs: visibility toggle eye icon, strength meter (4 segments), error message below. Naive implementations break at narrow viewports (icons overlap input, strength meter wraps awkwardly).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Visibility icon absolute-positioned without padding (text under icon)
Strength meter as 4 separate boxes with margin (wraps to 2 lines on narrow)
Error message that pushes form (CLS)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Input affordances (icon, meter) must coexist without overlapping.\n2. Strength meter is flex-distributed — automatically resizes.\n3. Error message position reserved (min-height) to prevent CLS.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Relative+absolute positioning for icon, flex strength meter
layout_strategy.content_priority: Input always functional; meter visible if password exists
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Same

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap eye icon (44pt) toggles visibility

performance_notes: Strength calculated on each input — micro-cost. flex-1 distributes meter segments evenly regardless of viewport. Reserved error min-height prevents CLS when error appears.
accessibility_across_viewports: aria-describedby links strength + error. role=progressbar with aria-value*. role=alert on error message. Eye toggle has aria-pressed. 44pt touch target on toggle (h-9 w-9 but inside an h-11 input with padding compensating).
edge_cases_handled: Very long password — input scrolls horizontally, eye icon stays visible
Visibility toggled — aria-pressed announces state to screen readers
Error appears — role=alert announces immediately, layout stable (reserved height)
Strength meter at narrow viewport — segments are narrow but visible
what_breaks_naive_approach: Absolute-positioned icon without input padding overlaps the password text. Strength meter with margins between segments wraps to 2 lines on narrow viewports. Error appearing pushes content down (CLS).
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Flex strength meter — works at any width. 2) Reserved error height prevents CLS. 3) Icon padding ensures no overlap with text at any size.

title: Background image with srcset via CSS image-set() that respects DPR and connection quality
viewport_spectrum: 1x/2x/3x DPR across mobile and desktop
scenario_context: Background image on a section. srcset is well-known. CSS image-set() does the same for background-image but is underused. Pair with prefers-reduced-data for connection-aware loading.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Single background-image url (always max size)
Hardcoding -webkit-image-set without standard fallback
Forgetting AVIF/WebP variants
responsive_reasoning: 1. CSS image-set() picks resolution/format like srcset does.\n2. Pair with prefers-reduced-data media query.\n3. type() inside image-set negotiates AVIF → WebP → JPEG.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: CSS image-set with type negotiation
layout_strategy.content_priority: Image visible; quality scales
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Same

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: N/A

performance_notes: Browser chooses optimal format/resolution from image-set — same logic as srcset. 1x users on slow networks (saveData) get smaller image.
accessibility_across_viewports: Background images are decorative — text contrast over them needs explicit handling. Use role=img + aria-label on parent if image conveys meaning.
edge_cases_handled: 1x DPR with AVIF support — picks hero.avif
3x retina iPhone — picks hero@3x.avif
Browser without AVIF — falls to WebP, then JPEG
User with saveData — single smaller image regardless of DPR
Older browser without image-set — uses fallback url
what_breaks_naive_approach: Single background-image url at desktop resolution forces mobile users to download 2MB. image-set is the CSS-native solution browsers have supported for years.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) CSS image-set for backgrounds — equivalent to srcset for . 2) Format negotiation via type(). 3) Data preference respected.

title: Command palette (Cmd+K) that becomes bottom sheet on mobile with input focused and keyboard auto-opened
viewport_spectrum: Desktop (centered overlay), tablet (centered, larger touch targets), mobile (bottom sheet with autofocus)
scenario_context: Linear/Vercel-style command palette. Desktop: Cmd+K opens centered modal with search input. Mobile: button in nav opens bottom sheet, input gets focus, keyboard appears automatically. Sheet results scroll independently.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Centered modal on mobile (cramped, hard to dismiss)
Search input that doesn't autofocus (forces extra tap)
Sheet that opens but keyboard doesn't auto-summon (poor mobile UX)
Trapping focus poorly (Tab escapes palette)
responsive_reasoning: 1. Command palette is power user feature — predominantly desktop. Mobile presence ensures parity but optimizes for thumb use.\n2. Bottom sheet feels native on mobile; sheet with input at top and results below.\n3. autoFocus on input + sheet open triggers iOS keyboard.

layout_strategy.primary_technique: Adaptive overlay with shared search and results
layout_strategy.content_priority: Search input always top; results scroll
layout_strategy.information_density_changes: Mobile shows fewer results per screen; desktop shows ~8

interaction_model_per_device.mobile_touch: Tap search button → sheet rises, keyboard appears, input focused, type to filter
interaction_model_per_device.tablet: Cmd+K (if keyboard attached) or button. Centered overlay.
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_mouse: Cmd+K shortcut, centered overlay, click to select
interaction_model_per_device.desktop_keyboard: Cmd+K open, arrow keys navigate results, Enter selects, Escape closes

performance_notes: Single global key listener. Filter runs on each keystroke (small dataset). Results virtualized in production for >100 commands. Portal avoids stacking issues.
accessibility_across_viewports: role=dialog aria-modal. Input aria-label. Listbox role with option items, aria-selected for active. Cmd+K shortcut visible only on devices with keyboards (via any-pointer:fine). Escape closes; Enter activates.
edge_cases_handled: Mobile sheet opens — autoFocus on input triggers iOS keyboard automatically
User types fast — selected resets to 0 when query changes (no stale selection)
Sheet covers most of screen — results scroll independently inside
No results — message displayed, doesn't break keyboard nav
User presses Cmd+K while palette open — no double-open
what_breaks_naive_approach: Centered modal on mobile is hard to thumb-reach. Not autofocusing input forces extra interaction. Trapping focus poorly lets Tab escape the palette into background content.
what_makes_this_truly_responsive: 1) Centered overlay on desktop, bottom sheet on mobile — native feel per platform. 2) autoFocus + sheet trigger iOS keyboard automatically. 3) Shortcut hint visible only when keyboard is attached.
